Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to expose PostgreSQL and S3 crash windows through an exhaustive workflow-state result; bound bytes, time, attempts, promises, streams, concurrency, cache, and spend; then deliver a duplicate or reordered event and prevent duplicate stakeholder effect. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. Node and AWS SDK packages, networks, S3, CloudFront, KMS, IAM, WAF, PostgreSQL, credentials, host state, handle and stream checks, load, faults, restore, billing, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: In a strict Node 24 and TypeScript 7 service, choose database-first, object-first, or direct-upload ordering and expose each crash window, compensation, and user-visible state.

For S3, CloudFront, and TypeScript Defend the Database Object Ordering model · Theory Database Object O…, challenge the documented misconception with runtime, file, object, policy, transfer, integrity, cache, fault, cost, restore, and controlled AWS transfer evidence: Putting the database write and SDK call in one TypeScript function makes them transactional. Type annotations, generated SDK shapes, and resolved promises do not strengthen that runtime claim.
